segments.s348-g153: The phrase is a pair of prepositional/locative phrases describing equipment: به زه بر کمان literally 'with/on the string upon the bow' and به بازو کمند 'and with a lasso on (his) arm'. In older Persian poetic usage به here marks an instrumental/locative relation; word order is preposition + object (به + instrument) followed by a secondary locative (بر کمان = 'on the bow'). The second half repeats the same pattern (به + body-part + object).
segments.s348-g154: A nominal clause composed of two coordinated noun phrases: سواری سرافراز 'a proud / exalted rider' and اسپی بلند 'a tall steed'. Adjectives follow their nouns (typical Persian order). و links the two coordinated nouns. اسپی is the older/poetic form of اسب (steed).
segments.s348-g155: همی رفت is the continuous/iterative past ('kept going' or 'was continually going'); همی is an imperfective/continuative adverb. هیشوی is an archaic/poetic negative/emphatic adverb meaning 'never' or 'not at all' and here combines with همی to convey 'kept going without ceasing' (an emphatic continuous motion). با او به راه is the prepositional phrase 'with him on/along the road' (با = 'with', به راه = 'on the road' or 'towards the road').
segments.s348-g156: This is a descriptive nominal cluster identifying the companion: جهانجوی (literally 'world-seeking') is an adjective/epithet, میرین is a proper name (Mīrīn), and فریاد خواه is a compound where خواه (from خواستن 'to want') functions adjectivally/participially to mean 'seeking/desiring a cry' — i.e. 'calling/cries for help'. Together they name the companion: 'Mirīn, the world-seeker, who cries out.'
segments.s348-g157: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so'. تا introduces a limit 'up to/until', and لب بیشه literally 'the lip/edge of the grove' (لب used as 'brink, edge'), so تا لب بیشه = 'as far as the edge of the grove'. فاسقون is a nominative plural noun 'the wicked/transgressors' and functions as the subject that the following verb (in the next segment) agrees with.
segments.s348-g158: برفتند is the simple past (3rd plural) 'they went/advanced'. پیچان (from پیچیدن) is a participial/adverbial form meaning 'winding' or 'twisting', describing their manner of movement. دل پر ز خون is a verbless predicative phrase 'heart full of blood' where پر ز (literally 'full of') governs the noun خون; the possessive 'their' is implied by the plural verb برفتند.
segments.s348-g9: A compound subject: the proper name 'کتایون' joined by و ('and') to 'آن مرد ناسرفراز' — 'آن' is the demonstrative/definite determiner 'that/the', 'مرد' = 'man', and 'ناسرفراز' is an epithet ('triumphant'/'proud') attached after the noun (typical poetic order).
segments.s348-g10: مرا (me + object marker را split here) is the direct object; داشتند is the 3rd person plural past of داشتن used as the main verb 'they held/kept'; از + چنان کار = 'from/of such a deed' (چنان = 'such'); باز here is part of the idiom 'باز داشتن' meaning 'to keep away / prevent' (an adverbial/lexical piece modifying the verbal idea). Word order places the verb after its object as often in Persian poetry.
segments.s348-g11: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; هرک is the classical contraction of 'هر که' = 'whoever'; جویند is a 3rd-person plural present (classical form) 'seek' used with the indefinite/relative هر که to mean 'whoever seeks'; خویشی من = 'my kinship' (object of جویند). The verb agrees generically with the indefinite subject.
segments.s348-g12: وگر = 'and if' / 'but if' (conditional particle). سر is the noun 'head' (here metonymically 'one's status/pride'); فرازد is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular form 'raises/exalts' (present/subjunctive/optative flavor in classical Persian used for hypothetical); به پیشی من = 'in/into precedence before me' ('پیشی' = precedence, with من marking comparison/contrast).
segments.s348-g13: یکی here functions as 'one / a certain' (indefinite) and introduces 'کار' ('a deed/work'). بایدش is the modal 'it must / is necessary for him' where the enclitic -ش refers to the third person (him/it); کردن is the infinitive 'to do' acting as the verbal noun, and بزرگ is the adjective 'great/large' describing the کار. The construction expresses necessity: 'one must do a great deed for him.'
segments.s348-g14: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; خوانندش is 3rd-person plural present 'they call it' (ش = object pronoun referring back to کار); ایدر = 'here'; بزرگان = 'the great ones / nobles' (the plural agent); سترگ is an epithet/adjective attached to describe those great ones (archaic/poetic word). Together: 'which the great ones here call ...'.
segments.s348-g15: چنو is an archaic/conjunctive 'چنان‌که' meaning 'as / since'; در جهان is the locative 'in the world'; نامداری is a substantive 'renown/fame'; بود is the past existential/copula 'was'. The clause says that renown existed in the world (contextually: 'I had renown in the world').
segments.s348-g16: مرا (again split as م + را) here marks the person affected ('for me' / 'I had'); بر زمین is a locative 'on the earth/land'; نیز = 'also'; یاری = 'help / ally(s)'; بود = past 'there was' — classical Persian uses 'مرا یاری بود' to mean 'I had help.' Word order places the object/beneficiary before the predicate 'بود'.
segments.s348-g89: کنون is an adverb 'now'. سوی من is a prepositional phrase 'toward me' (من as object 'me'), then the verb کرد appears before its subject (poetic inversion): کرد ... میرین = 'Mirīn did/turned' and together with the following noun پناه ('refuge') the phrase means 'sought refuge'. In short: 'Now Mirīn turned toward me and sought refuge.'
segments.s348-g90: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain / someone'. نامدارست is a predicate 'is famed'. با دستگاه is an adjunct 'with (his) دستگاه' — دستگاه here means the ruler's court/retinue, i.e. 'with a retinue/pomp'. The clause states 'a certain famed one (is) with a retinue.'
segments.s348-g91: دبیرست is a nominal predicate meaning 'he is a دبیر (secretary/scribe)' — the -ست is an archaic copula-like form. با دانش 'with knowledge' and و ارجمند 'and esteemed' are adjectival/qualifying phrases modifying that subject; word order places the copula-like predicate first ('is a scribe ...').
segments.s348-g92: بگیرد is a present/epic-form verb 'he takes/keeps' (verb before object here by poetic order). شمار is the direct object 'the number/count'. سپهر بلند 'the lofty heavens/sky' is a genitival/qualifying phrase for شمار ('the count/number of the lofty heavens'). So: 'he keeps/counts the number of the high heavens.'
segments.s348-g93: سخن گوید is a verbal phrase 'he speaks / utters speech' (سخن as object/topic, گوید the verb). از marks the theme/topic 'about' or 'of' (گوید از ... = 'speaks of ...'), so 'he speaks of the philosophers of Rome.'
segments.s348-g94: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from / of' introduces the source/scope. آباد و ویران are paired adjectives 'inhabited and ruined', and هر مرز و بوم = 'every border and land / every region'. The whole phrase means 'from every inhabited and ruined border and land' (i.e. everywhere).
segments.s348-g95: هم 'also' introduces the clause. از marks source 'from/of' (هم از ... = 'also from/of ...'). گوهر سلم literally 'the gem of Salm' names the provenance; سلم here is a proper name (a person), so none of the provided common-word senses fit. دارد نژاد is 'has lineage' (دارد = 'has/possesses' and نژاد = 'lineage'); the verb follows its object in the poetic order but functions normally: 'his lineage is from the gem of Salm.'
segments.s348-g96: پدر بر پدر is a repetitive genitival/appositive expression 'father upon father' (= 'from father to father / from generation to generation'). بر here is the preposition 'upon/on'. نام دارد به یاد places the predicate: نام دارد 'has a name / is named' and به یاد 'in remembrance' (i.e. 'his name is remembered'). The clause means 'from father to father his name is remembered.'
segments.s348-g113: The phrase begins with the prepositional adverb 'بدو' (to him), followed by the past-tense verb 'گفت' (said). The name 'گشتاسپ' follows the verb in a topicalized/inverted order—literally ‘to him said Gushtasp.’ The clause closes with the predicate 'کری رواست' where 'کری' functions as the noun/adjective ‘deafness/being deaf’ and 'رواست' (rava-st) is a compressed copula + adjective meaning ‘is permitted/fitting.’
segments.s348-g114: This is a compound interrogative/personal question. 'چه گویند' is literally ‘what do they say’ (imperfect/gnomic present sense). The coordinating 'و' links the next direct question 'این بیشه اکنون کجاست'—‘where is this grove now?’ Word order is normal for Persian questions: interrogative ('چه' / 'کجاست') precedes the verb/copula 'ست'.
segments.s348-g115: 'چگونه' introduces an exclamatory/interrogative 'how' or 'in what way.' The noun 'ددی' here denotes a monstrous/terrible being (archaic/poetic) and 'باشد' is the present/subjunctive copular/auxiliary meaning ‘can be / may be.’ 'اندر جهان' is the locative phrase ‘in the world.’ Altogether: 'How can such a creature be in the world?'
segments.s348-g116: This relative clause begins with 'که' (‘that/which’) and contains the verb 'ترسند' (present plural, from 'ترسیدن') meaning ‘they fear/revere.’ 'ازو' is the old unstressed form 'from/of him.' The coordinated noun phrase 'کهتران و مهان' lists social ranks (‘the lesser and the great’), so the clause reads ‘that the lowly and the great fear him.’
segments.s348-g117: 'چنین گفت' is a formulaic opening ‘thus said,’ with 'چنین' (‘thus’) and the past verb 'گفت' (‘said’). 'هیشوی' is a proper name or epithet (here the speaker), and 'کاین پیر گرگ' is a reduced relative/nominal phrase ‘that this old wolf’ (ک + این = ‘that/this’; 'پیر' = 'old'; 'گرگ' = 'wolf'), serving as the subject of what follows.
segments.s348-g118: 'همی' is a frequentative/emphatic particle meaning ‘indeed/still,’ followed by the adjective 'برتر' (‘higher/superior’) and the copula 'است' (‘is’). The preposition 'از' introduces the standard of comparison 'هیونی سترگ' — literally ‘from savage/feral creatures’ (archaic poetic nouns). The clause reads ‘(he) is indeed superior to ferocious beasts.’
segments.s348-g119: This simile uses parallel structure: 'دو دندان او' (‘his two teeth’) followed by 'چون' (‘like/as’) and the comparison 'دو دندان پیل' (‘the two tusks of an elephant’). Word order is possessor after possessed (dandan-o = his teeth). 'پیل' (= elephant) is the standard compared to.
segments.s348-g120: Another parallel description: 'دو چشمش' (‘his two eyes’) + a nominal phrase 'طبر خون' where 'طبر' (broad axe) modifies metaphorically and 'خون' (blood) intensifies the imagery—i.e. eyes like bloody axes. The conjunction 'و' links the next clause 'چرمش چو نیل' (‘his hide like the Nile’) where 'چو' = 'like' and 'نیل' evokes a dark-blue/black color (the Nile or indigo) in poetic usage.
segments.s348-g121: سروهاش is سرو + ـَش (his cypresses), a poetic metaphor (tall, slender limbs). چو introduces a simile here ('like'). آبنوسی is an adjective meaning 'ebony/ebony‑colored', placed after the simile marker as in classical Persian poetry. فرسپ (steed) follows as a nominal image completing the metaphor; word order is noun (his cypresses) + simile + adjective + noun image.
segments.s348-g122: چو here is temporal ('when'); خشم آورد is a light‑verb expression where آورد literally 'brought' is used idiomatically with خشم to mean 'anger came/seized him'. بگذرد is present/subjunctive/indicative third‑person singular used here in a gnomic/past‑narrative sense 'he would pass' or 'he passes'; بر + دو اسپ literally 'over two horses' (بر = over/above). The clause says that when anger seized him he would pass over two horses (i.e. stride/powerfully over them).
segments.s348-g123: از ایدر = از + ایدر ('from here'); بسی is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'much/many' or 'a great number', modifying قیصران; نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; قیصران is the plural 'Caesars/emperors'. Word order is prepositional phrase (from here) + quantifier/adverb + adjective + noun.
segments.s348-g124: برفتند is the past plural of رفتن with the prefix ب‑ (here narrative past 'they went/set out'); با introduces an instrument/possession 'with'; گرزهای is the plural/construct of گرز 'mace' (گرز + ـهای plural), and گران is the adjective 'heavy' (or 'valuable') modifying گرزهای. The phrase literally: 'they went with heavy maces.'
segments.s348-g125: ازان بیشه = ازان (از + آن, 'from that') + بیشه ('grove, thicket'); ناکام is an adjective 'unsuccessful/thwarted'; باز آمدند is the past plural 'they returned' (باز = again/back; آمدند = they came). The phrase states that they came back from that grove unsuccessful.
segments.s348-g126: پرِ از ... آمدند is a verbless descriptive cluster attached to آمدند: پر (here 'full (of)') + از ('of') + ننگ ('shame'), then و connecting تن پر گداز: تن ('body') + پر ('full (of)') + گداز (here abstract 'ardor/anguish, burning pain'). In short: 'they came back full of shame and with bodies full of burning anguish.'
segments.s348-g127: بدو is the dative 'to him' (be +  ـو), marking the indirect object 'to him'; گفت here functions as the narrative 'said' (the printed sense list is nominal but in context it is the verb 'said'); گشتاسپ is the speaker's name (Gushtasp). کان is an archaic demonstrative here ('that' — poetically used to point to a specific item) and تیغ سلم = 'the sword of Salm' (تیغ = sword, سلم = proper name Salm). Word order: indirect object + گفت + speaker + (demonstrative + object).
segments.s348-g128: بیارید is an imperative plural 'bring (it)' directed to attendants (here: 'bring (them)'); و = 'and' links the two commands/objects; اسپس (pl. of اسپ) = 'horses/steeds'; سرافراز is an adjective 'honored/proud/triumphant' modifying اسپس; گرم here is an adjective meaning 'fierce/ardent/eager' describing the horses. Together: 'Bring ... and proud, eager horses.' (Note: بیارید is the classical imperative; the senses listed with the lemma are not the semantic fit here, so sense index for بیارید is set to -1.)
segments.s348-g49: ز here is the poetic preposition 'از' meaning 'about/of'; the phrase کار کتایون is the noun phrase 'Katayun's affair(s)'. خود is an emphatic/reflexive 'himself' (emphasising the subject), and آگاه بود is the past-tense copular form meaning 'was aware'. Word order is typical: prepositional/nominal phrase + emphatic pronoun + predicate ('was aware').
segments.s348-g50: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. با is the preposition 'with'. نیو here functions as a companion/name/instrument in the clause (the lexical entry(s) given do not clearly include a proper-name reading, so its exact lexical sense is uncertain). گشتاسپ is the proper name (Gushtasp). همراه بود is the compound predicate 'was accompanied' / 'was a companion' (همراه as noun/adjective + بود). The clause thus reports who accompanied whom.
segments.s348-g51: ز again functions as the poetic 'از' ('of/from'). هیشوی is a proper name here (not the adverbial 'never'), so none of the listed senses fit exactly. و is the coordinating 'and'. آن مهتر is 'that elder/chief' (آن = 'that', مهتر = 'chief/elder'). نامجوی is a personal name (or epithet); taken here as a name.
segments.s348-g52: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'. هر سه is an idiomatic classical Persian way to mean 'the three of them' / 'all three'. به روی اندر آرند روی is a somewhat duplicated, poetic expression: به روی + اندر (toward the face / into the presence) + آرند (they bring/turn — third person plural form) + repeated روی for emphasis ('their faces'). آرند here is a plural present/past-subjunctive/historic form used in narrative.
segments.s348-g53: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. به نزدیک = 'to/near' (toward the vicinity of). هیشوی تفت appears to be a name or place compound ('Hishoy Taft' or 'Hishoy, Taft'); the provided lexical senses for هیشوی and تفت do not include a proper-name/place reading, so those are treated as names here.
segments.s348-g54: سراسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout' or 'all over' and modifies the verb. بگفت is the past-tense narrative 'he said/told' (the b- prefix is the classical/periphrastic form). آن سخنها = 'those words'. که رفت is a relative clause 'that went/that had gone' — رفت used here in the sense of 'had been uttered/was said' or 'had passed'.
segments.s348-g55: وزان is a fused connective 'و + از آن' meaning 'and/from that' or 'and because of that'. اختر is 'star' (often an omen). فیلسوفان روم = 'the philosophers of Rome' (noun phrase: philosophers + genitive/locative 'Rome'). The phrase points to the agents who react to the omen.
segments.s348-g56: شگفتی is the noun 'wonder/astonishment'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. آید is the present/subjunctive 'may come' or literary present 'comes' used to report the expected event. ب + دان in the word list are the elements of بدان = 'to that' (a prefixed preposition + demonstrative); here they function together as 'to/into that'. مرز و بوم is a common collocation 'border and land' = 'that country/territory'.
segments.s348-g81: نشستی here functions as a noun meaning 'a sitting/seat/assembly' (an archaic nominal form derived from نشستن). آراست is a past/perfective form of آرستن 'to adorn/arrange' (third-person singular), so نشستی نو آراست = 'a new seat/assembly was adorned/arranged'. بر پیش آب is a prepositional phrase where بر + پیش together mean 'in front of / before' and آب is the locative object 'the water'. Word order is noun + adjective (نشستی نو) followed by the verb and the locative phrase.
segments.s348-g82: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain'; خوان is 'banquet/table (for a feast)'; ساخت is the past tense 'made / set up' (third-person singular); اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste' with اندر = 'in/within' and شتاب = 'haste'. The whole clause means 'someone set up a new banquet quickly.'
segments.s348-g83: می‌آورد is the imperfective/narrative-form of آوردن 'to bring' (here rendered as 'he brought / was bringing' in narrative); با is the preposition 'with'; میگساران is the plural agent 'cupbearers / wine-attendants' (with the plural ending implied); نو modifies میگساران 'new'. The phrase describes bringing company for the feast.
segments.s348-g84: نشستی again denotes the 'sitting/assembly' or 'seating' (nominal use), نو 'new' modifies آیین 'rite/ceremony/custom' and the coordinated phrase و یاران نو adds 'and new companions'. Note the adjective follows the noun (آیین نو / یاران نو) as is common in older Persian, and the clause states that a new ceremonial seating and new companions were present.
segments.s348-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; رخ is 'face/cheek' and is the subject; لعل here denotes the ruby/deep-red color (so رخ لعل = 'cheek turned ruby'). گشت is the past form of گشتن 'to become/turn' (= 'became'). از before می‌لعل فام expresses cause 'from/because of'; می‌لعل فام is a compound describing the wine's ruby hue ('mi-lal' = wine of ruby color, فام = hue). The clause reads 'when the cheek became ruby because of the ruby-hued wine.'
segments.s348-g86: به marks the indirect object 'to/for' and introduces گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) as the addressee. هیشوی here appears in the text as an element immediately before گفت; in context it functions as the naming/agent slot (likely a proper name or vocative in the manuscript) — grammatically the main verb is گفت 'said' (past tense), followed by ای (vocative particle 'O') and همام (a proper name or title). The sequence = 'Heishav said to Gushtasp, "O Homam."' (Note: هیشوی's lexical list in the prompt does not supply a matching sense, so it is treated as a name/agent here.)
segments.s348-g87: مرا is the accusative 'me' (object); بر زمین = 'upon the earth / in the world' (بر + زمین); دوست خوانی is second-person singular present/persistent sense of خواندن 'to call / to name' (subject 'you' understood) so 'you call (me) friend'; همی is the habitual/continuous particle used in classical Persian to indicate an ongoing or customary present ('do/are'), so the clause means 'you call me friend on earth.' Note the classical order places the object before the verb.
segments.s348-g88: جز از من = 'except from me' (جز = 'except'); کسی را = 'anyone' with the object marker را (kasi rā); ندانی is second-person present negative of دانستن 'to know' ('you do not know'); همی again adds habitual/continuous aspect. Together: 'you know no one except me.'
segments.s348-g137: بسی هدیه بگزید is a past‑tense clause: بگزید is the 3rd person singular past of گزیندن (‘to choose’), with its object (هدیه) placed before the verb; بسی (quantifier) precedes the noun to mark abundance. با آن ز گنج is a circumstantial/prepositional phrase: با آن (‘with that’ / ‘from that’) followed by ز + noun (poetic ‘from’) indicates source (from that treasure). Word order reflects Persian tendency to put objects and modifiers before the final verb.
segments.s348-g138: ز introduces a list of items taken from the treasure. یاقوت و گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase (‘rubies and gems’), and همه پنج‌پنج is a postpositive comment: همه (‘all’) + پنج‌پنج (an idiom/adjective meaning ‘completely/entirely in good order’) qualifies the listed jewels.
segments.s348-g139: چو is a comparative/temporal conjunction here (‘like/as’). خورشید is the simile term (‘the sun’), and پیراهن قیرگون is the noun + adjective (‘a pitch‑black robe’), with the adjective following the noun as usual in Persian. The clause prepares an action likened to the sun removing a dark covering.
segments.s348-g140: بدرید is the simple past (3rd sing.) of دریدن (‘to tear’); here it governs پیراهن. The conjunction و links a second simple past clause آمد (‘he came’), and ز پرده برون is a postverbal prepositional phrase meaning ‘out from the curtain’ (ز = poetic ‘from’, پرده ‘curtain’, برون ‘out’).
segments.s348-g141: جهانجوی is an epithet (‘world‑seeking’/‘ambitious’) placed before the proper name میرین (here a personal name), forming a noun phrase that acts as the subject. ز ایوان is a source/locative phrase (‘from the iwan/portico’). برفت is a past‑tense verb meaning ‘went/left’; it appears sentence‑finally as typical in Persian.
segments.s348-g142: بیامد is another past‑tense verb (‘came’). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to/toward near’ (i.e. ‘approached’). هیشوی تفت is a compact phrase where هیشوی functions adverbially/attributively before تفت (‘scorching/heat’); the exact lexical force of هیشوی is obscure here (see senses).
segments.s348-g143: ز نخچیر is a prepositional phrase (‘from the spoil/game’). گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). زانسو follows as the direct object (a proper name) and کشید is the past‑tense verb (‘drew/took/pulled’ here in the sense ‘drew out/took out’); the clause shows a somewhat inverted order (source phrase first) typical of poetic diction.
segments.s348-g144: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction: نگه (look/gaze) + کردن = ‘to cast a glance’ or ‘to look’. هیشوی appears again as an adverbial/particle here (its lexical sense is uncertain in the supplied list). و اورا بدید is coordinated: و (‘and’) + اورا (object pronoun ‘him’) + بدید (past ‘saw’), with the object placed before the verb as is common.
segments.s348-g97: به نزدیک ... : a prepositional phrase meaning 'near' or 'close to'. به + نزدیک together mark proximity ('near to'). اویست here functions as a fused/archaic copula phrase meaning 'is (to) him' or simply 'is (near him)'—an older/poetic form linking the locative phrase to the noun. شمشیر سلم is a noun phrase 'the sword of Salm' (possessive understood from adjacency).
segments.s348-g98: که بودی همه‌ساله در زیر سلم: که introduces a relative clause 'which'. بودی is an older/past copula 'was' (past tense). همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'every year' / 'annually'. در زیر = 'in/under' (در + زیر) yields 'under'; سلم again names Salm. The clause describes where/when the sword used to be.
segments.s348-g99: سواریست گردافکن و شیر گیر: سواریست is a nominal phrase 'he is a rider/horseman' (est/ʼast fused in poetry). گردافکن is a descriptive compound 'dust‑scattering' or 'dust‑raising', i.e. a fierce charger. و links to شیر گیر, where شیر 'lion' + گیر 'catcher/seizer' = 'lion-catcher' (one who seizes lions).
segments.s348-g100: عقاب اندر آرد ز گردون به تیر: عقاب 'eagle' is direct object/target; اندر آرد is a verbal phrase 'brings/downcasts/puts into' (here 'brings down'); ز گردون = 'from the sky/heaven' (ز = from, گردون = sky/fate); به تیر uses به in an instrumental/societal sense 'by/with an arrow' (poetic usage). The verb آرد is 3rd‑person present/past poetic form 'brings'/'brought'.
segments.s348-g101: برین نیز خواهد که بیشی کند: بر + ین (برین = بر + این) = 'on this / regarding this'. نیز = 'also/likewise'. خواهد is the modal/auxiliary 'will/wishes' (future/intent). که introduces a subordinate clause; بیشی کند = 'to do more / to increase / to excel' (بیشی 'more/greater' + کند 'does'). The whole unit says he will, moreover, wish to do more about this.
segments.s348-g102: چو با قیصر روم خویشی کند: چو = 'when/if/as' (here temporal/conditional 'when' or 'if'); با = 'with'. قیصر روم = 'the Caesar of Rome' (a title + place). خویشی = 'kinship/affinity' (forming a marital or political tie). کند = 'does/makes'—so 'when he makes kinship with the Caesar of Rome.'
segments.s348-g103: به قیصر سخن گفت و پاسخ شنید: به قیصر = 'to the Caesar' (prepositional object). سخن گفت is the idiom 'spoke (words)' or 'addressed (someone)'; گفت here is the speech verb in poetic phrase. و پاسخ شنید = 'and heard a reply' (شنید = 'heard'). The sequence narrates that he spoke to the emperor and received an answer.
segments.s348-g104: ز پاسخ همانا دلش بردمید: ز = 'from'/'because of' (poetic). پاسخ = 'the answer/reply'. همانا = an interjection 'verily / behold'. دلش = 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش). بردمید is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'was carried off / seized away'—figuratively 'his heart was carried away' (i.e., he was deeply moved or enraptured).
segments.s348-g105: که introduces reported speech ('that'); او گفت is the reporting clause 'he said' with گفت as the past narrative verb. در بیشه فاسقون is a prepositional phrase 'in the thicket/grove of the fāsiqūn (the wicked)' where در precedes the noun phrase and فاسقون is an Arabic plural used as a genitive/attributive noun following بیشه.
segments.s348-g106: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one' introducing an existential; گرگ is the noun 'wolf' and باشد is the optative/subjunctive form of بودن (here expressing possibility: 'may be' / 'might be'). بسان is a comparative preposition 'like, as', and هیون (a poetic/archaic form) means 'beast, savage creature'.
segments.s348-g107: This is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر 'if'. کشته آید is a poetic/passive construction: کشته (past participle) + آید (present/formal verb) yields 'be killed / should be killed'. به دست تو marks the agent/instrument with the preposition به + 'hand' + the possessor تو (literary 'by your hand'). The final گرگ is the logical subject/affected noun ('a wolf'). Note the old-fashioned word order (participle + verb + agent + subject).
segments.s348-g108: تو باشی is a jussive/subjunctive-like prediction 'you shall be / you will be' (باشی used as a future/predicative). به روم is a locative/directional phrase 'at/to Rome' and ایرمانی بزرگ is a nominal phrase 'a great Armenian' with ایرمانی (Armenian) modified by بزرگ 'great'. The order is literary: predicate-following locative then noun phrase of status.
segments.s348-g109: جهاندار باشی uses جهاندار 'sovereign/ruler' as a predicative noun and باشی as the same jussive/predictive 'you will be'. و links the clauses; داماد من is a possessive noun phrase 'my son‑in‑law' where من marks the possessor. The phrase states two honors conferred on the addressee.
segments.s348-g110: This clause is a short wish or reflection: زمانه here means 'fortune / the world' rather than simply 'age'. به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness / kindly' (به + خوبی), دهد is the present/factual verb 'gives', and داد من is 'my due / my right / my justice' (داد used as 'right' or 'just share'). The construction is compact and somewhat formulaic: 'May Fortune, kindly, give my due.'
segments.s348-g111: کنون‌گر is a literary conditional adverbial 'now if' (introducing a present conditional). تو این را کنی uses the subjunctive/potential کنی 'if you do' with را marking این as the definite object. دست پیش is an idiomatic noun phrase 'the first move / the initiative' (literally 'hand before'), placed after the conditional clause for emphasis.
segments.s348-g112: منت بنده‌ام is a set phrase meaning 'I shall be indebted / your obliged servant' (منت = favor/obligation, بنده‌ام a humble self-reference). وین سرافراز خویش is a compact phrase: وین 'and this', سرافراز 'proud/honored', and خویش+ش (here written as خوی + ش) forming the reflexive/possessive 'one's own' — thus 'and this proud one shall be mine.' The clause ties the promise of service to the speaker's claim over the proud person/thing mentioned.
segments.s348-g33: Word order is emphatic/poetic: the subject pronoun 'من' appears at the start, followed by the direct object 'این چاره' and the adverb 'اکنون'. The verbal phrase 'بجای آورم' is a classical/perfective-stative construction meaning 'to effect/accomplish' (بجای + آوردن); here 'آورم' is first-person present-subjunctive/optative used to state the speaker's intention ('I will accomplish').
segments.s348-g34: The preposition 'ز' (poetic) = 'from/of' introduces the source/extent ('ز هرگونه'). 'هرگونه' modifies the following noun 'رای' (any kind/sort of counsel). 'پاکیزه' is an adjective qualifying 'رای'. The verb 'آورم' repeats the same first-person form and here means 'I will bring/produce/present' that counsel; the clause gives the content of what the speaker will accomplish.
segments.s348-g35: 'چو' here = 'when' (temporal subordinating particle). The verb 'آمد' is simple past 'came'; the prepositional phrase 'به ایوان پسندیده' ('to the pleasing/approved portico') precedes the subject noun 'مرد' due to poetic inversion — the normal prose order would be 'مرد به ایوان پسندیده آمد'.
segments.s348-g36: The preposition 'ز' (poetic) introduces the domain 'هرگونه اندیشه‌ها' = 'thoughts of every kind'. 'یاد کرد' is a compound verbal expression: the noun 'یاد' ('remembrance') combines with the light verb 'کرد' to mean 'he remembered' or 'he called to mind'; 'کرد' is past tense, so the whole clause is in past narrative.
segments.s348-g37: The noun 'نوشته' ('a writing/text') is the object; 'بیاورد' here is narrative (past/result) 'brought' — in classical/poetic Persian the present-subjunctive form can serve narratively as a past. The conjunction 'و' links the next verb 'بنهاد' (past of نهادن) meaning 'placed/put down'. 'پیش' is the preposition 'before/in the presence of' and marks the indirect object/location.
segments.s348-g38: A noun phrase enumerating items with 'و': 'همان' ('that very/the same') modifies 'اختر' ('star'), then 'طالع' ('fate/ascendant') and 'فال' ('omen'). The final element is the possessive 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his own', so the whole phrase means 'that very star and his fate and omen'. Word order is straightforward listing.
segments.s348-g39: 'چنان دید' = 'he saw thus' or 'he had such a vision'; 'دید' is past tense 'saw'. 'کاندر' (= 'کـ' + 'اندر' fused) functions like 'in which/therein' and introduces the time phrase 'فلان روزگار' = 'some particular time' or 'a certain day/age'. The clause sets up a prophetic vision.
segments.s348-g40: The prepositional phrase 'از ایران' = 'from Iran' marks origin. The verb 'بیاید' is a present-subjunctive/optative often used for future-oriented or prophetic sense here ('will come' / 'may come'). 'یکی نامدار' = 'a certain renowned one' (یکی used as 'someone/a certain person', 'نامدار' = 'famous').
segments.s348-g41: به دستش برآید is a poetic idiom: the preposition به + دستش (“to/into his hand”) + the verb برآید (literary present/impersonal 'come/appear') together mean 'fall into his hands' or 'become his to do'. The following three words (سه کار گران) are the logical object: 'three heavy/important deeds/tasks' (کار as 'deed/work', گران as 'grave/difficult'). Word order is inverted compared with colloquial Persian but typical of Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s348-g42: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' referring back to the preceding clause. باز is the adverb 'again' and گویند is the 3rd-person plural present ('they say' / 'they will say') — here reporting that the Roman leaders will speak of those deeds. رومی سران is a poetic inversion meaning 'the Roman chiefs/leaders' (modifier before noun).
segments.s348-g43: یکی انک is an emphatic/poetic way to introduce 'one (namely that)...': یکی 'a certain/one' and the archaic demonstrative انک 'that'. داماد قیصر is a noun phrase 'the Caesar's son‑in‑law' (داماد 'son‑in‑law/groom' with possessor قیصر). شود is the literary present/future 'becomes' — 'he becomes Caesar's son‑in‑law.'
segments.s348-g44: همان picks up 'that same (man)'. بر سر قیصر is the locative phrase 'on the head of the Caesar' (بر سر = 'upon the head of'). افسر شود uses افسر (here 'helmet') with شود; idiomatically this reads 'he causes/puts a helmet on Caesar's head' or 'a helmet is set upon Caesar's head (by him)'. The verb is literary and the construction is somewhat laconic/poetic.
segments.s348-g45: پدید آید is a literary inversion of 'آید پدید' meaning 'appear/come forth' (پدید 'visible/manifest' + آید 'comes'). از روی کشور literally 'from the surface/face of the country' = 'out of the land/realm'. دو دد = 'two enemies' (دد 'hostile ones/enemies').
segments.s348-g46: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'such that / who'. هرکس رسد is 'whoever arrives/comes' (هرکس 'whoever', رسد literary present 'reaches/comes upon'). از بد دد به بد is terse/poetic: از + (بد دد) 'from the evil/hostile enemy' and به بد 'to bad (state)' — the phrasing means those who encounter them fare from bad to worse under their hostility.
segments.s348-g47: شود is the literary copula 'will be / will happen'. هردو ('both') is the subject. بر دست او is an agentive/instrumental phrase 'by his hand' (i.e. by him), and بر هلاک 'into destruction / to ruin' indicates the outcome: 'both will come to ruin by his hand.' The verb is in the same poetic, elevated register.
segments.s348-g48: ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from'. هر زورمندی 'any/mighty one' (هر 'every/any' + زورمندی 'might/strong one'). نیایدش is the negated present 'does not come to him' (ni- + آید + pronoun enclitic) meaning 'he will not feel/receive' — here 'he will not experience/need fear'. باک 'fear' is the noun complement: 'he will have no fear of any powerful man.'
segments.s348-g73: چو introduces a temporal clause here (‘when’). میرین is a proper name and the subject; بدیدش is the past tense verb ‘saw’ with the object pronoun -ش ‘him’. به + هیشوی marks the indirect object ‘to Hīshūy’ (said to Hīshūy). گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’ completing the reporting clause.
segments.s348-g74: که is the complementizer introducing reported speech (‘that’ / ‘that …’). این را is the demonstrative plus the definite-object marker (را) pointing to the person just seen. به گیتی is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the world’ (به here marks the domain). کسی نیست جفت is a negated existential statement: کسی نیست ‘there is no one’ and جفت (‘match/peer’) is the predicate noun meaning ‘equal/match for him.’
segments.s348-g75: بدین is a compressed prepositional expression meaning ‘with these/by these’ (ب- prefix + این). The following coordinated nouns شاخ, یال and دستبرد enumerate the features ‘horn, mane, and (hand-)seizure’ linked by و ‘and’. Word order places the instrument/feature list before the copula that follows in the next segment.
segments.s348-g76: ز is the poetic ablative/preposition ‘from’ or ‘of’. تخمی functions as an adjectival/attributive phrase meaning ‘seed‑born’ or ‘of seed/origin’. بود is the past copula ‘was’. نامبردار and گرد are predicate adjectives joined by و; نامبردار here means ‘renowned/famed’ and گرد functions adjectivally (here glossed as ‘about/around’ or possibly ‘rounded’ depending on sense).
segments.s348-g77: هنرها is the plural subject ‘the arts/skills’. ز دیدار او uses ز + noun to mean ‘by/at the sight of him’. بگذرد is a present/general form ‘pass(es)/surpass(es)’ (ب‑ prefix + present stem) meaning ‘are passed/surpassed’ in the sense ‘are put to shame/are eclipsed’. The clause states that the arts are outdone by seeing him.
segments.s348-g78: همان here acts as a resumptive or emphatic demonstrative meaning ‘such/namely’. A tricolon lists qualities: شرم (shame/modesty), آزردگی (hurt/annoyance), و خرد (and wisdom). خرد here is the abstract noun ‘wisdom’ rather than ‘small’. The list names the things that are overcome or affected.
segments.s348-g79: چو again marks a temporal clause ‘when’. گشتاسپ is the proper name Goshtāsp (subject). تنگ آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase ‘became constrained/pressed’ (تنگ ‘narrow’ + آمد ‘came’), meaning he was put in a difficult situation. این هر دو مرد is a postposed phrase ‘these two men’ (این + هر دو = both).
segments.s348-g80: پیاده ب‌‌بودند is a compound past: پیاده ‘on foot’ + ببودند (past plural ‘they were’) — ‘they were on foot’. ز اسپ is an ablative/‘from the horse’ or ‘without horses’ phrase. نبرد here refers to the fighting/battle (noun) so the clause conveys that they were on foot and (thus) lacking horses for the fight.
segments.s348-g145: ازان is a fronted demonstrative/adverb meaning “of those/thereupon” that sets the reference for what follows. اسپ و شمشیر are the coordinated nouns (horse and sword) that function as the grammatical subject, and خیره شد(ند) is a predicative adjective + past auxiliary meaning “were astonished/stood agape.” The plural auxiliary شدند marks the resultant state for the two items.
segments.s348-g146: چو introduces a temporal clause (“when”); نزدیک‌تر is the comparative form of نزدیک (made with -تر) meaning “nearer/approaching,” and شد is the past of شدن “became.” پذیره is a participle/noun meaning “reception/accepted,” and شد(ند) again functions as the past auxiliary making a passive/resultative “were accepted.” The clause therefore reads: “when (he) became nearer, (they) were accepted.”
segments.s348-g147: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). آن هدیه‌ها is the definite/demonstrative object “those gifts,” with آن qualifying هدیه‌ها. بنگرید is an older/polite past form of نگرستن (“to look, to gaze”); the verb closes the clause, typical of Persian SOV order in classical style.
segments.s348-g148: همان is an emphatic demonstrative “that very / the same.” اسپ و تیغ are the coordinated direct objects “horse and sword.” از میان is the prepositional phrase “from among,” and برگزید is the past tense verb “chose/selected.” Note the object(s) appear before the verb—normal in Persian—and the verb picks out those items from the group just mentioned.
segments.s348-g149: دگر چیز is a noun phrase “other things” (دگر = other). بخشید is the past verb which here means “gave/bestowed” (not ‘forgave’). هیشوی را is the indirect object marked by را; in context this looks like a proper name or recipient. The object marker را confirms هیشوی is definite/particular.
segments.s348-g150: بیاراست is a past verb meaning “adorned / made beautiful” (classical form). جان is the noun “life/soul” and جهانجوی is a compound noun meaning “world‑seeker / ambitious person,” here modifying جان (the life of the world‑seeker). را again marks the definite object. Word order places the verb before its object phrase in this short clause.
segments.s348-g151: بپوشید is a past form of پوشیدن and here means “he put on / clothed (himself).” In classical Persian the verb can precede the subject (here گشتاسپ). خفتان is a noun meaning “sleep,” and چو گرد is a small temporal/figurative phrase “when (it) turned/rounded (around) / when sleep came about him.” The sequence expresses: he dressed himself and, when sleep closed about him...
segments.s348-g152: به زیر اندر آورد is a verb phrase meaning “brought into/underneath” (به + زیر + اندر + آورد, with اندر = archaic ‘in’). اسپ نبرد is a noun phrase where نبرد functions as a modifier of اسپ (“war/ battle” + horse → war‑horse); thus the whole clause says he brought the war‑horse under/near him. Classical Persian often stacks direction words before the verb as here.
segments.s348-g57: بدو گفت is literally “to him (بدو) said (گفت)” — the indirect object phrase precedes the verb as is common in Persian (‘‘he said to him’’). کامروز is a temporal adverb “this very day/today,” and شاد is a predicative adjective (“happy/joyful”) placed before the imperative in poetry for emphasis. هیشوی is an archaic/adverbial particle (difficult in classical usage); here it functions as an emphatic/time-related adverb modifying the admonition to be joyful.
segments.s348-g58: بر ما همی باش is an imperative construction: بر ما = “toward us / with respect to us,” همی is an older continuous/intensifying particle (meaning roughly “still/always” or a durative marker), and باش is the imperative ‘be.’ با مهر و داد is a prepositional phrase complementing the imperative: با = “with,” مهر = “kindness/love,” و داد = “and justice.”
segments.s348-g59: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause describing این مرد (“this man”). کز وی = “from whom” (کز = from/whence + وی = him), and تو دادی is the 2nd-person past ‘you gave.’ نشان (placed after the verb in poetic order) is the direct object “a token/sign.” The clause literally reads: “that this man, from whom you gave a token.”
segments.s348-g60: یکی is an indefinite “a certain/some,” نامداریست is a nominal predicate meaning “is famed/renowned” (نامدار + -یست, copular), and از marks membership or source “of/among.” سرکشان is the plural noun (here contextually ‘the chiefs/defiant ones’); in poetry the noun following از gives the class to which the subject belongs.
segments.s348-g61: به نخچیر دارد literally “he has/keeps for the game” (به + نخچیر), where دارد is the present/persistent verb “has/keeps.” همی is the durative/continuous particle (“continually”), and روی و رای are two nouns in apposition — “face/appearance (روی)” and “counsel/plan (رای)” — functioning as the things he keeps or reserves for the hunt. Word order places the objects after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s348-g62: نیندیشد is a negative form meaning “he did not / does not think/consider” (classical negative formation with نی-). از introduces the object of thought “about/of,” and تخت خاور خدای is a compressed noun phrase: تخت = “throne,” خاور = “eastern/of the east,” and خدای = “God” (here attributive/possessive: “the eastern throne of God”).
segments.s348-g63: یکی دی نیامد به نزدیک من — in this context یکی with the negative verb functions as “no one/not one.” دی = “yesterday” (time adverb) and نیامد is the past negative “did not come.” به نزدیک من = “near me / to my presence.” The usual subject–time–verb order appears (subject + time + negative past verb), with the prepositional phrase following.
segments.s348-g64: که introduces the explanatory clause “that/because ….” خرم شدی is a two-word sequence: خرم “joyful” plus شدی (here second-person past/causative sense) — taken together in poetic usage as “you made (it) joyful / you caused to be happy.” جان تاریک من is the object “my dark soul” (جان = soul/life; تاریک = dark/gloomy; من = my). The object follows the verb for emphasis, so the clause means “that you have made my dark soul joyful.”
segments.s348-g17: شود تا is a literary optative/jussive: 'may it be that' or 'let it happen that'; تا here introduces the wished-for clause ('so that/may'). 'سر بیشه' is a genitival/locative phrase meaning 'the head/top of the grove' or more poetically 'at the head of the thicket'; 'فاسقون' is a plural attributive ('the wicked'). The whole segment sets up the wish that follows.
segments.s348-g18: بشوید is a present-subjunctive/passive-style verbal form here meaning 'may ... be washed' (literary optative/passive). دل و دست و مغزش is a coordinated list of internal parts ('heart and hand and his brain') where مغزش is the possessed form ('his brain'). به introduces the medium/agent ('in/with blood'). The clause completes the wish begun by شود تا.
segments.s348-g19: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'someone/sometimes' ('one/at times'). گرگ is the noun 'wolf' and بیند is the (literary) present tense 'sees' used to state an observed example. به + کردار = 'in the manner/guise of'; نیل here is the color/indigo metaphor ('like indigo' — a poetic simile for appearance).
segments.s348-g20: This is a compound predication with دارد ('has') governing two possessions: تن اژدها 'the body of a dragon' (noun + appositive/genitive image) and زور پیل 'the might/strength of an elephant'. The conjunction و links the two parallels; word order places the possessed/image before دارد in a vivid descriptive style.
segments.s348-g21: سرو دارد is a poetic attribute: 'he has (the) cypress' (i.e., cypress-like stature/tallness). The conjunction و links the next image: نیشتر ('point'/'lancet' or 'tusk/point') qualified by چون گراز 'like a boar' — a simile comparing that point/tusk to a boar's.
segments.s348-g22: This clause is compressed/archaic and reads like a negative/result clause about elephants: شدن is the infinitive 'to become' and پیشش فراز 'rise up/stand up before him on high'. The verb form نیارد is an archaic/poetic item here functioning negatively or as a modal (i.e. 'does not come/does not rise'), so the whole yields 'an elephant does not (can not) rise before him on high.' (The lexical entry supplied for نیارد does not match this usage and is marked below.)
segments.s348-g23: بران ('on/over that' or 'upon that') + بیشه ('grove') gives the locative 'over/at that grove'. بر نگذرد is a negative-styled present 'does not/cannot pass' (literary present used as general truth). نره شیر = 'male lion' (نره modifying شیر). The clause states that even a male lion does not pass there.
segments.s348-g24: Parallel negative coordination: نه ... و نه ... = 'neither ... nor ...'. The first term is پیل (elephant). The second coordinated noun phrase خونریز مرد دلیر stacks adjective + noun + epithet: خونریز 'blood‑shedding/bloody' modifies مرد 'man', and دلیر 'brave' is an attributive epithet; together 'no elephant, and no bloodthirsty brave man'.
segments.s348-g1: This is a verbless/existential clause in past narrative order: 'یکی ... بود' = 'there was a certain ...'. میرین is a personal name placed after the copula بود; به نام is a prepositional phrase head (here completed by the next segment). Word order is free/poetic: 'one Roman was Mirīn by name.'
segments.s348-g2: This continues the phrase begun by 'به نام' in the previous segment: سرافراز is the epithet/name applied to him ('Sarafraz'). The following و بارای is a poetic linking (بارای here an archaic preposition meaning 'for/in respect of'), and با گنج و کام lists possessions/attributes ('with treasure and desire').
segments.s348-g3: فرستاد is a simple past verb 'he sent/dispatched'; نزدیک قیصر functions as the goal ('to/near Caesar', i.e. to the emperor's presence); پیام is the direct object 'a message'. The clause is compact: 'he sent a message to Caesar.'
segments.s348-g4: که introduces reported speech ('that/saying that'). من is the first-person subject. سرفرازم is an adjectival predicate with the enclitic -م ('I am sarfarāz/proud/distinguished'). به گنج و به نام uses به + noun in parallel to mean 'with regard to wealth and with regard to name' — i.e. 'in wealth and in reputation/fame.'
segments.s348-g5: This is an imperative request: 'به من ده ... را' = 'give to me ... (object marked by را)'. دل‌آرام (here a proper or pet-name 'Del-aram' / 'beloved') modifies دخترت ('your daughter'); را marks the definite object. The omitted explicit verb 'ده' (give) is understood in the clause.
segments.s348-g6: Another imperative: 'به من تازه کن' = 'make fresh/renew for me'. تازه is 'new/fresh' and کن is the imperative of کردن ('do/make'). نام و افسرت را are the objects: 'name and afsar(=diadem/helmet)' (افسرت = afsarat/afser + clitic), with را marking them definite objects.
segments.s348-g7: چنین گفت قیصر is a fixed narrative formula 'thus said Caesar.' که introduces the reported clause. زین سپس is an archaic temporal expression ('from this/thereafter' = 'from now on'); here زین is poetic/archaic and pairs with سپس ('then/afterwards').
segments.s348-g8: نجویم is the first-person (subjunctive/negative-able) form of جویدن/جویدن? here 'to seek / to look for' (with an implied negative 'ن' = 'I will not seek' in context). The following tokens are a split 'بدین' (here given as ب + دین) meaning 'by/with this' — the split in the wordlist prevents a clean sense-match, so دین does not correspond to the listed senses. روی پیوند کس = 'a match/alliance (پیوند) in this manner (روی) with any person (کس)'.
segments.s348-g129: همی is an archaic/aspectual particle here that gives a present/iterative sense ("I now/ever"). اژدها خوانم is a verb phrase with خوانم (present/archaic 1st‑person) meaning "I call/name" (not "read"); این را is the definite object (این = "this", را = object marker). The final گرگ after a negation (نه ... گرگ in the larger line) is the contrasted noun "wolf."
segments.s348-g130: تو is the addressed "you." گرگی is an adjectival/relational form "wolf‑like / as a wolf." مدان is an archaic or obscure form here (contextually negative/imperative or exhortatory) and functions to tell the addressee not to assume/consider something. از + هیونی بزرگ gives the origin/qualifier "from/of a great beast," so the clause warns the addressee not to take himself for a wolf born/of a great monster.
segments.s348-g131: چو here = "when." بشنید is a past tense passive/3rd‑person form "he heard" (or "it was heard" depending on subject). میرین in this line is a proper name (Mīrīn), the subject who reacts. زانجا = "from there/thence." برفت is an older past form meaning "he went off/left," placed at the end of the clause as the main past action.
segments.s348-g132: سوی = "toward" marking destination; خانه خویش is a possessive phrase "his own house" with خوی + ش (kinship/dispositional stem + pronominal suffix) forming خویش. تازید is the past/telic verb "he rushed/rode off quickly." The final تفت is an adverbial or intensifying word here (archaic usage) strengthening تازید — "sped fiercely/with force."
segments.s348-g133: ز آخر is a poetic phrase meaning "at the end / finally." گزین کرد is the compound verb "chose / made a choice" (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made), followed by اسپی سیاه = "a black horse/steed." Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase → verb phrase → direct object.
segments.s348-g134: گرانمایه is an adjective/noun meaning "valuable/precious" modifying the following item (here a gift or piece of equipment). خفتان in the manuscript appears as a noun (though the listed senses show a verbal root); here it functions as an item name (likely a saddle or similar horse‑equipment in context). و is the coordinating "and," followed by رومی کلاه = "a Roman (Byzantine) helmet/hat."
segments.s348-g135: همان is a demonstrative "that/same." مایه‌ور is an adjective meaning "wealthy/valuable" or "richly adorned" modifying تیغ ("sword"). الماس گون is a compound where الماس = "diamond" and گون (‑گون) is a suffix/word meaning "—like / -colored," so الماس‌گون = "diamond‑like / diamond‑colored." The phrase as a whole describes the sword's appearance and value.
segments.s348-g136: که introduces a relative clause "which/that." سلم here is the proper name Salm (the character who performed the action). دادش is the compound verb "gave it / put on it" (داد + third‑person object suffix), functioning as past tense "he gave/caused to be" — in context meaning "he soaked/treated it." به زهر و به خون is a serial prepositional phrase "with poison and with blood," specifying what was given to / applied to the sword.
segments.s348-g25: هر آنکس که ... is a conditional relative: 'whoever' (هر آنکس که) introduces a protasis. بر وی uses the preposition بر + the clitic وی meaning 'against/on him'. بدرید is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'tears / rends' (used here as the action in the protasis). پوست is the direct object 'skin' placed after the verb in this terse, elevated word order.
segments.s348-g26: مرا باشد او ... is the apodosis: مرا باشد literally 'be to me' = 'shall be mine' with the clitic را split as a separate token; باشد is the classical subjunctive/coptula meaning 'be / shall be'. The subject او follows the verb (stylistic inversion) and یار و داماد و دوست are predicate nouns linked by و ('and') giving a triadic apposition: 'ally, son‑in‑law, and friend'.
segments.s348-g27: چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'), with گفت the verbal past/statement marker; میرین is a proper name serving as the speaker and follows the verb (typical verb–subject order in classical Persian). بر + ین + زادبوم here marks direction/target — 'to/at that homeland' (بر + the following element functioning as a demonstrative/adjective + زادبوم 'homeland').
segments.s348-g28: جهان‌آفرین is a vocative/exclamatory address 'O Creator (world‑maker)'. تا introduces purpose/optative 'so that / may', پی 'in pursuit of / on the track of', and افگند is an archaic/poetic past/optative 'cast / throw' giving the wish 'may [he/the Creator] cast [me] after Rūm'; روم is the object (Rūm = Byzantium/Rome).
segments.s348-g29: نیاکان ما 'our ancestors' (noun + possessor). جز به is a fixed instrumental/exclusive phrase 'except by / except with', where به takes the instrumental sense 'by'. گرز گران is a noun + adjective ('mace heavy' = 'heavy mace') with the adjective following the noun as usual in Persian.
segments.s348-g30: نکردند is the past negative 'they did not do / they did not wage' (نـ prefix + کرد in classical narrative), followed by the object/nominal پیکار 'battle'. با introduces the opponent 'with', and مهتران 'the great/greater ones' (here 'chiefs/leaders') names whom they did not battle except as stated earlier.
segments.s348-g31: کنون 'now' sets the time. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar/emperor'. از من بجوید همی literally 'seeks from me' — بجوید is the present/habitual form (ب‑ prefix in epic Persian) meaning 'seeks/asks about', and همی adds continual/habitual force ('keeps on / continually').
segments.s348-g32: سخن با من 'words / speech to me' (سخن + با + من). از کینه is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from hatred' meaning 'with hateful intent' or 'in hatred'. گوید همی is the present/habitual 'he says / he keeps saying' (گوید with همی marking continuous action). The whole line: 'he continually speaks to me with words born of hatred.'
segments.s348-g65: "بیاید" is an optative/3rd-person subjunctive meaning "may he come" (verb-first as often in older/poetic Persian). "هم‌اکنون" is an adverb "right now", and the prepositional phrase "ز نخچیرگاه" (= from the hunting-ground) follows to mark origin. The whole chunk is a simple verb + temporal adverb + source phrase: "May he come now from the hunting-ground.".
segments.s348-g66: This clause gives the result/state: "بما" = "for us/to us", "بر" here functions as a directional/relational particle ("toward/with respect to"), and "بود" is past tense "was". "بی‌گمانیش" is an adverbial/ emphatic form of "بی‌گمان" = "without doubt/undoubtedly", and "راه" (placed at the end) is the nominative/predicate noun "the way/path." Word order is somewhat inverted (predicate after verb) but yields: "for us the way was, undoubtedly."
segments.s348-g67: The verb "آورد" (past "brought") comes at the end, typical in Persian. The coordinated nouns before it (here written "می‌و رود") function as the things brought — a pair of nouns (fruit/wine or fruit and a musical item, depending on poetic usage). "با بوی و رنگ" is an instrumental/adverbial phrase "with scent and color," modifying how/what was brought. Overall: "(He) brought X with scent and color." (poetic coordination and elliptical items are common).
segments.s348-g68: "نشستند" is the past plural "they sat" (here meaning they took their seats). "با جام زرین" = "with a golden cup" ("جام" = cup, modified by adjective "زرین"). "به چنگ" is an idiomatic locution meaning "into/into the grasp" i.e. "in hand"; note that "چنگ" literally means "harp" or "claw/talon," and here functions figuratively as "grasp/hand." The phrase describes their posture: "they sat with a golden cup in hand.".
segments.s348-g69: "هم انگه" (also written "هم آن گاه") = "just then/at that moment." "که شد" introduces the event/time (here: "when it came to pass"). "جام می‌بر" is an agentive label: "جام‌می‌بر" = "the cup-bearer/one who takes the cup." Word order is compressed/poetic: together the segment means "just then, when the cup-bearer (was about to) take the cup..." with "شد" marking the occurrence.
segments.s348-g70: "پدید آمد" is a two-word verbal phrase meaning "appeared" or "came into view" ("پدید" = appear, "آمد" = came). "از دشت" = "from the plain," and "گرد سوار" describes the coming group: "گرد" here functions adverbially/collectively ("round/about" → a band), and "سوار" = "rider/horseman." Together: "a band of horsemen appeared from the plain."
segments.s348-g71: "چو" = "when." The next two items are proper names: "هیشوی" and "میرین" (Hishuy and Mirin) as the subjects. "بدیدند" is the past plural "they saw." The final word "گرد" (= "around/encircling") is an adverbial/participle describing what they saw: "When Hishuy and Mirin saw (them) around/encircling..." Note: one of the provided lemma-senses lists treats "هیشوی" as an adverbial word, but here it functions as a name, so select the name sense when reading the verse.
segments.s348-g72: "پذیره شدندش" = "they received him/they welcomed him" — "پذیره" (reception/shelter/acceptance) plus the past plural auxiliary "شدند" with the enclitic object pronoun "-ش" = "him/it." "به دشت نبرد" is a locative/directional phrase: "into/to the plain of battle" ("به" = toward/into, "دشت" = plain, "نبرد" = battle). So the clause says they took him in/received him into the battlefield/plain.
